events.text: The patient is a 71-year-old male named Robert Delacroix. He is of French-Canadian descent, retired civil engineer. He is a former smoker (30 pack-years, quit at age 55), non-drinker, with a family history significant for his father dying of prostate cancer at age 78 and a paternal aunt with breast cancer at age 62. He lives with his wife and has two adult children.

At age 68 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IVB (AJCC 8th edition, cT3bN1M1b) Prostatic Adenocarcinoma, acinar type. Primary site: Prostate gland (C61.9). Histology: Acinar adenocarcinoma (8140/3). Gleason score: 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5. PSA at diagnosis: 87.4 ng/mL. Bone metastases present at initial diagnosis (pelvis, lumbar spine, right femur). Pelvic lymph node involvement confirmed. Relevant biomarkers: AR positive, PSA positive. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy (12 cores). Tumor Size: Bilateral involvement, estimated 80% of gland. Perineural invasion: Present. Extraprostatic extension: Present. Seminal vesicle invasion: Present (bilateral). Clinical stage: cT3bN1M1b.

At age 68 years, the patient had a pathology result of type TRANSRECTAL ULTRASOUND-GUIDED PROSTATE BIOPSY (12-core). Gross description: Twelve cores submitted in separate labeled containers. Microscopic findings: Cores 1-4 (right base/mid): Acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason pattern 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5), involving 70-100% of each core. Perineural invasion present. Cores 5-8 (left base/mid): Acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason pattern 4+4=8 (Grade Group 4), involving 40-80% of each core. Cores 9-12 (bilateral apex): Acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason pattern 4+5=9, involving 60-90% of each core. Immunohistochemistry: PSA positive, PSMA strongly positive, AR positive, CK5/6 negative (confirming adenocarcinoma). Pathologic diagnosis: High-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, involving all 12 cores bilaterally.

At age 68 years, the patient had a TECHNETIUM-99M BONE SCAN which showed multiple areas of increased radiotracer uptake consistent with osseous metastatic disease. Sites of involvement: bilateral sacroiliac joints, L2, L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, right femoral neck and proximal shaft, left posterior rib (7th). No evidence of superscan. Impression: Extensive polyostotic skeletal metastatic disease. Cancer status: Cancer present with osseous metastatic involvement.

At age 68 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST which showed prostate gland enlarged and heterogeneous, estimated volume 65 cc, with invasion of seminal vesicles bilaterally. Pelvic lymphadenopathy: right obturator node 2.1 cm, left external iliac node 1.4 cm. No visceral metastases identified (liver, lung parenchyma, adrenal glands unremarkable). Lytic/sclerotic mixed bony lesions in pelvis and lumbar spine consistent with osseous metastases. Impression: Locally advanced prostate cancer with pelvic nodal involvement and bone metastases. No visceral disease. Cancer status: Cancer present, bone and nodal metastases without visceral involvement.

At age 68 years, the patient received androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) initiated with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg intramuscular injection every 3 months (LHRH agonist), combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily plus prednisone 5 mg twice daily, for metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC). Treatment intent: disease control/life-prolonging. Castrate testosterone levels confirmed at 4 weeks (testosterone < 20 ng/dL). PSA declined from 87.4 to 3.2 ng/mL at 3 months.

At age 68 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment 3 months after initiating ADT + abiraterone/prednisone. Findings: PSA declined from 87.4 to 3.2 ng/mL (96% decline). Testosterone confirmed castrate at 14 ng/dL. Patient reports fatigue (grade 1), hot flashes (grade 2), and mild bilateral lower extremity edema (grade 1). Performance status ECOG 1. Weight 89 kg. Cancer status: Cancer present but responding to therapy. Plan: Continue current regimen, add zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 3 months for bone-directed therapy. Begin calcium 1200 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU daily.

At age 68 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for prostatic adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the prostate biopsy (FFPE tissue). Germline testing also performed via peripheral blood. Somatic findings: TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 42%, pathogenic), PTEN deletion (homozygous loss), CDK12 p.K1213fs*6 mutation (VAF 18%), RB1 splice site mutation (c.607-2A>G, VAF 31%), PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (VAF 12%). Copy number alterations: AR amplification (copy number 8), MYC amplification (copy number 12), PTEN deletion (biallelic). No BRCA1/BRCA2 somatic mutations detected in tumor (germline separate). Fusions: None detected. MSI status: MSS (microsatellite stable). TMB: 4 mutations/Mb (low). PD-L1 IHC: 1% (CPS). Germline results (separate blood-based panel): BRCA2 pathogenic variant c.5946delT (p.Ser1982Argfs*22, known pathogenic) confirmed heterozygous germline mutation. BRCA2 loss of heterozygosity confirmed in tumor (somatic second hit). This finding has implications for PARP inhibitor eligibility. Genetic counseling referral placed. All other HRR genes (ATM, CDK12 somatic, BRCA1, PALB2, FANCA) reviewed - no additional germline HRR variants.

At age 69 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment at 12 months on ADT + abiraterone/prednisone. PSA nadir achieved at 0.8 ng/mL (month 9). Current PSA: 1.1 ng/mL (slight rise from nadir but not yet meeting PCWG3 criteria for progression). Testosterone castrate at 11 ng/dL. Bone scan repeated: stable osseous disease, no new lesions. ECOG PS 1. Patient tolerating regimen well. Hot flashes persistent grade 2. Reports mild cognitive sluggishness. Cancer status: Cancer present, on treatment, disease stable. Plan: Continue ADT + abiraterone/prednisone, repeat PSA in 8 weeks.

At age 70 years, the patient had a TECHNETIUM-99M BONE SCAN at 18 months of ADT + abiraterone therapy which showed known osseous metastases in lumbar spine, pelvis, right femur stable without significant interval change. No new bone metastases identified. Soft tissue uptake not suspicious. Impression: Stable skeletal metastatic disease. Cancer status: Cancer present, osseous metastases stable.

At age 70 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment documenting castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) transition. PSA progression: PSA values over preceding 3 months: 1.8, 3.4, 6.7 ng/mL, confirming CRPC by PCWG3 criteria (two consecutive PSA rises, each >= 25% above nadir and >= 2 ng/mL, confirmed while testosterone castrate < 50 ng/dL). Testosterone: 9 ng/dL (confirmed castrate). ECOG PS 1. No symptomatic progression. Abiraterone/prednisone discontinued. Given known germline BRCA2 mutation, patient is eligible for PARP inhibitor therapy. However, enzalutamide chosen as next-line given tolerability profile and to preserve olaparib for later use (PSA rising but no radiographic progression). Discussed clinical trial options - no appropriate trials currently open at this institution. ADT (leuprolide) continued. Cancer status: Cancer present, biochemically progressing on abiraterone, now meeting CRPC definition.

At age 70 years, the patient received enzalutamide 160 mg orally daily for castration-resistant prostate cancer (post-abiraterone). Treatment intent: disease control/life-prolonging. Concurrent ADT (leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3 months) continued. Bone-protective therapy with zoledronic acid continued quarterly.

At age 70 years, 2 months after starting enzalutamide, the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA declined from 6.7 to 2.1 ng/mL (69% decline). Testosterone castrate at 12 ng/dL. Patient reports fatigue grade 2, mild dizziness (grade 1), and increased falls risk identified on gait assessment. Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor discontinued. Hot flashes worsened to grade 3. ECOG PS 1. Weight: 91 kg. Cancer status: Cancer present, responding to enzalutamide. Plan: Continue enzalutamide 160 mg daily. Physical therapy referral for fall prevention.

At age 70 years, approximately 3 months after starting enzalutamide, the patient experienced a grade 2 fatigue adverse event (CTCAE v5.0: Fatigue, Grade 2) and grade 1 dizziness/lightheadedness. Additionally, enzalutamide-related seizure risk counseling provided. No dose reduction required at this time. Patient also developed grade 1 hypertension (new onset, systolic 148 mmHg) managed with amlodipine 5 mg daily.

At age 70 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST at 4 months on enzalutamide which showed no new visceral lesions. Liver, lungs, adrenal glands unremarkable. Pelvic lymph nodes: right obturator node decreased from 2.1 cm to 1.3 cm (partial response). No new lymphadenopathy. Bony lesions stable, sclerotic changes suggestive of treatment response. Cancer status: Cancer present, partial response in lymph nodes, stable osseous disease.

At age 71 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment at 6 months on enzalutamide. PSA nadir achieved: 0.6 ng/mL at month 5. Current PSA: 0.9 ng/mL. Testosterone castrate. ECOG PS 1. Patient tolerating well, fatigue grade 1-2, stable. Cancer status: Cancer present, best PSA response achieved (93% decline from pre-enzalutamide baseline). Disease stable on imaging. Plan: Continue enzalutamide, repeat bone scan in 3 months, PSA every 6-8 weeks.

At age 71 years, the patient had a TECHNETIUM-99M BONE SCAN at approximately 9 months on enzalutamide which showed new areas of increased radiotracer uptake in T10 and T12 vertebral bodies and right posterior 4th rib, consistent with new bone metastases. Previously known osseous metastases in lumbar spine, pelvis, right femur show mixed picture with some lesions more prominent than prior scan. Impression: Disease progression with new bone metastases. Cancer status: Cancer present, radiographic progression with new osseous metastases.

At age 71 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment documenting radiographic and biochemical progression on enzalutamide. PSA progression: 0.6 (nadir) to 1.4, 3.8, 9.2 ng/mL over 3 months. New bone metastases on bone scan (T10, T12, right 4th rib). Testosterone castrate at 8 ng/dL. ECOG PS 1-2. Patient reports new thoracic back pain (grade 2), radiating around the right chest wall. Spine MRI ordered to rule out cord compression. Given BRCA2 germline status and post-second-generation ARSI progression, now meets criteria for olaparib (PARP inhibitor). Enzalutamide discontinued. Plan: Initiate olaparib. Continue ADT. Refer to radiation oncology for palliative radiation to T10/T12 for pain control. Cancer status: Cancer present, progressing on enzalutamide.

At age 71 years, the patient had a MRI THORACIC/LUMBAR SPINE WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST which showed osseous metastatic lesions at T10 and T12 vertebral bodies with associated epidural soft tissue extension at T10, causing mild-to-moderate spinal canal narrowing (approximately 35% reduction) without cord signal change. T2 level: No cord compression. Right 4th rib lesion confirmed. L2, L3, L4 metastases with no epidural extension. Impression: T10 epidural disease with moderate canal compromise, not meeting criteria for emergent intervention but warrants palliative radiation. No cord compression. Cancer status: Cancer present with epidural extension at T10.

At age 71 years, the patient received palliative external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) to T10 and T12 vertebral metastases for pain control and prevention of cord compression. Technique: 3D-conformal radiation therapy. Dose/fractionation: 20 Gy in 5 fractions (4 Gy per fraction) to T10-T12 spinal field. Treatment completed over 5 consecutive weekdays. Concurrent olaparib held for 1 week during radiation per institutional protocol. Plan: Resume olaparib following radiation completion. Response: Pain improved from grade 2 to grade 1 at 2-week follow-up.

At age 71 years, the patient received olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily for castration-resistant prostate cancer with germline BRCA2 mutation (post-enzalutamide and post-abiraterone progression). Treatment intent: disease control/life-prolonging, based on PROfound trial data. Concurrent ADT (leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3 months) continued. Zoledronic acid bone-protective therapy continued quarterly. Baseline CBC: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, WBC 6.2 x10^9/L, Platelets 198 x10^9/L.

At age 71 years, 8 weeks after initiating olaparib, the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA declined from 9.2 to 3.1 ng/mL (66% decline). Testosterone castrate. ECOG PS 1. Patient reports fatigue grade 2 (worsened from baseline), nausea grade 1 (managed with ondansetron PRN). Hemoglobin declined to 9.6 g/dL (from 11.8 g/dL at baseline) - grade 2 anemia. No bleeding. Cancer status: Cancer present, responding to olaparib (PSA decline). Anemia emerging, likely olaparib-related myelosuppression. Plan: Monitor CBC weekly x 4 weeks, then every 2 weeks. Nutritional assessment. Continue olaparib at current dose. Add iron supplementation.

At age 71 years, approximately 10 weeks after starting olaparib, the patient experienced grade 3 anemia (CTCAE v5.0: Anemia, Grade 3; Hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL). Symptoms: marked fatigue, dyspnea on exertion (grade 2), palpitations. Olaparib held temporarily. Patient received packed red blood cell (pRBC) transfusion: 2 units pRBC administered. Post-transfusion hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL. Symptoms improved. Olaparib dose reduced to 200 mg twice daily per prescribing information guidelines for grade 3/4 hematologic toxicity. Bone marrow suppression attributed to olaparib mechanism (inhibition of PARP-mediated DNA repair in hematopoietic progenitors). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent considered but deferred given oncologic context.

At age 71 years, the patient had a PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) at approximately 4 months on olaparib which showed marked decrease in PSMA avidity in known osseous metastases in lumbar spine, pelvis, right femur compared to prior bone scan (treatment response). T10, T12 lesions with post-radiation changes, low PSMA uptake. No new osseous lesions. No visceral PSMA-avid lesions identified. Pelvic lymph nodes: No PSMA-avid nodes. Impression: Substantial metabolic response to olaparib in osseous metastatic disease. No visceral metastases. Cancer status: Cancer present, responding to olaparib, osseous disease with marked metabolic response.

At age 71 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment at 4 months on olaparib (at reduced dose 200 mg BID). PSA: 1.4 ng/mL (continued decline from 9.2 pre-olaparib). Testosterone castrate at 10 ng/dL. Hemoglobin: 9.2 g/dL (stable anemia grade 2). Patient has required total of 4 pRBC transfusions in the past 3 months. ECOG PS 1-2. Fatigue remains grade 2. PSMA PET shows excellent metabolic response. Cancer status: Cancer present, responding to olaparib. Transfusion-dependent anemia ongoing but manageable. Plan: Continue olaparib 200 mg BID, transfusion support as needed, CBC every 2 weeks.

At age 72 years, the patient experienced recurrent grade 3 anemia episodes requiring additional packed red blood cell transfusions. Over a 5-month period on olaparib, patient received a cumulative total of 8 units pRBC across 4 separate transfusion episodes. Hemoglobin levels ranged 7.4-8.2 g/dL at transfusion thresholds. Erythropoietin level checked: 38 mIU/mL (appropriately elevated, excluding EPO deficiency). Bone marrow biopsy deferred. Renal function normal (creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 64 mL/min). No evidence of hemolysis (normal LDH, haptoglobin, reticulocyte count low at 0.8%). Assessment: Olaparib-related bone marrow suppression causing hypoproliferative anemia. Olaparib dose maintained at 200 mg BID given ongoing oncologic benefit.

At age 72 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST at approximately 8 months on olaparib which showed new hepatic lesions: two hypodense liver lesions, segment 5 (1.8 cm) and segment 7 (1.2 cm), not present on prior imaging, consistent with hepatic metastases. New bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 8mm, left lower lobe 6mm, new since prior CT. Osseous metastases: stable to slightly increased in size in lumbar spine and pelvis. Pelvic lymph nodes: right obturator node increased to 1.9 cm (was 1.3 cm). Impression: Radiographic progression with new visceral metastases (hepatic and pulmonary) and nodal progression. Cancer status: Cancer present, progressive disease with new visceral metastases - hepatic and pulmonary.

At age 72 years, the patient had a pathology result of type LIVER BIOPSY (CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY, SEGMENT 5 LESION). Gross description: Three core needle biopsy fragments, tan-white, firm. Microscopic findings: Cores replaced by infiltrating carcinoma with glandular architecture. Tumor cells with prominent nucleoli, abundant pale cytoplasm, arranged in acini and cribriform structures. Immunohistochemistry: PSA positive (strong, diffuse), PSMA positive (strong), NKX3.1 positive, CK7 negative, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, TTF-1 negative, AR positive. Ki-67 proliferation index 45%. Morphology: Adenocarcinoma consistent with metastatic prostatic origin. No neuroendocrine differentiation (synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative). Pathologic diagnosis: Metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma to liver. Histology unchanged from primary (acinar adenocarcinoma). No evidence of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation.

At age 72 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the liver biopsy (FFPE tissue, metastatic site). Somatic findings: TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 58%, same as primary, enriched), PTEN deletion (homozygous, confirmed), CDK12 p.K1213fs*6 (VAF 34%), RB1 splice site c.607-2A>G (VAF 45%), PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 22%). New alterations not seen in primary: AR splice variant ARv7 detected (RNA-based assay, positive), MET amplification (copy number 18, high-level amplification), AKT1 p.E17K mutation (VAF 8%). BRCA2 biallelic loss confirmed (germline + somatic LOH, maintained from primary). Copy number alterations: AR amplification (copy number 14, increased from 8), MYC amplification (copy number 16), PTEN deletion. MSI status: MSS. TMB: 6 mutations/Mb (low). CDK12 biallelic inactivation confirmed (CDK12 mutations may predict immunotherapy benefit). No new actionable fusions. AR-V7 positivity predicts resistance to androgen receptor signaling inhibitors. MET amplification is a potential acquired resistance mechanism and possible therapeutic target.

At age 72 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment documenting visceral disease progression on olaparib. PSA: 18.4 ng/mL (rising from nadir of 1.4 ng/mL). New hepatic and pulmonary metastases confirmed on CT and liver biopsy. Testosterone castrate at 7 ng/dL. ECOG PS 2. Hemoglobin: 8.1 g/dL (requires transfusion). Liver function tests: ALT 48 U/L (grade 1 elevation), bilirubin normal, alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L (mildly elevated). Weight loss 6 kg over 3 months. Patient with decreased appetite and worsening fatigue. AR-V7 positive on ctDNA liquid biopsy (confirmatory). Given visceral crisis not imminent but visceral progression on PARP inhibitor post 2nd-gen ARSI, discussing next line options: cabazitaxel-based chemotherapy, lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (PSMA PET positive), docetaxel, pembrolizumab (CDK12 biallelic, low TMB but CDK12 potential predictor), or clinical trial. Patient declining chemotherapy at this time. Palliative care consultation placed. Cancer status: Cancer present, progressing on olaparib with visceral metastases. Olaparib discontinued.

At age 72 years, the patient had a PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) at time of olaparib progression which showed multiple new hepatic PSMA-avid lesions (at least 4 lesions throughout right and left lobes, maximum SUV 12.4). Bilateral pulmonary nodules with PSMA avidity (right lower lobe SUVmax 6.2, left lower lobe 4.8). Progressive osseous metastases with increased PSMA avidity throughout lumbar spine, pelvis, right femur, new lesions in thoracic spine. Right obturator and external iliac lymph nodes with increased PSMA uptake. Impression: Widespread PSMA-avid disease with visceral, nodal, and osseous progression. Patient eligible for lutetium-177 PSMA therapy based on PSMA expression. Cancer status: Cancer present, progressive visceral, nodal, and osseous disease.

At age 72 years, the patient received lutetium-177 (Lu-177) PSMA-617 (vipivotide tetraxetan) 7.4 GBq IV every 6 weeks for castration-resistant prostate cancer with visceral metastases (post-olaparib, post-enzalutamide, post-abiraterone). Treatment intent: disease control/life-prolonging, based on VISION trial data. Concurrent ADT (leuprolide) continued. Zoledronic acid continued. Baseline: PSA 18.4 ng/mL, Hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL (post-transfusion), creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, salivary gland protection with amino acid infusion during each cycle.

At age 72 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment after 2 cycles of Lu-177 PSMA-617. PSA: 11.2 ng/mL (39% decline from 18.4 ng/mL baseline). Hemoglobin: 8.6 g/dL (stable, no transfusion required this cycle). Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL (stable). Patient reports mild dry mouth (xerostomia, grade 1), fatigue grade 2. ECOG PS 2. Appetite slightly improved. CT restaging planned after cycle 4. Cancer status: Cancer present, early biochemical response to Lu-177 PSMA therapy. Palliative care actively co-managing symptom burden. Goals of care conversation initiated - patient and family informed of incurable nature of disease, focusing on quality of life and time at home.

documents.text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Genitourinary Medical Oncology

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
NP/PA: [Advanced Practice Provider]

PATIENT: Robert Delacroix
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
DIAGNOSIS: Metastatic castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, AR-amplified

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Delacroix is a 71-year-old French-Canadian male (now age 70 at this visit) with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) diagnosed approximately 24 months ago, presenting today for follow-up and disease reassessment. He has been on continuous ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID since diagnosis. He is here today because of a rising PSA trend identified on his last three blood draws, raising concern for castration-resistant progression.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Delacroix initially presented approximately two years ago with a PSA of 87.4 ng/mL, bone scan demonstrating extensive polyostotic skeletal involvement (pelvis, lumbar spine, right femur, bilateral sacroiliac joints), and CT showing pelvic lymphadenopathy. Biopsy confirmed high-grade acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5). He was promptly started on leuprolide 22.5 mg IM every 3 months plus abiraterone/prednisone under the LATITUDE/STAMPEDE framework for mHSPC.

Initial response was excellent — PSA fell from 87.4 to 3.2 ng/mL by month 3, reaching a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at approximately month 9. Bone scan at the 18-month mark showed stable osseous disease without new lesions. Testosterone has remained consistently castrate throughout treatment (most recently 9 ng/dL on today's labs).

However, over the past three clinic visits spanning roughly 12 weeks, PSA has risen as follows: 1.8 → 3.4 → 6.7 ng/mL. Each value was drawn with a confirmed castrate testosterone. The trajectory clearly meets PCWG3 criteria for biochemical progression in the castrate setting — two consecutive rises, each ≥25% above the PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL, with absolute PSA ≥2 ng/mL, on confirmed castrate testosterone. This represents a transition to castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC).

Importantly, he does NOT have radiographic progression at this time. Bone scan from 6 weeks ago showed no new osseous lesions. He has not had interval CT imaging, which we will plan today. Symptomatically, he denies new bone pain, neurologic symptoms, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. He reports ongoing grade 1-2 fatigue and persistent hot flashes but no new complaints referable to disease progression.

As context for treatment planning: NGS performed on his original prostate biopsy specimen confirmed a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) with confirmed loss of heterozygosity in tumor — making him a strong candidate for PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib per PROfound data). Somatic profile also notable for TP53 p.R248W, biallelic PTEN loss, CDK12 frameshift, RB1 splice site mutation, and AR amplification (copy number 8 at time of biopsy). AR-V7 status has not yet been assessed from a current liquid biopsy specimen but will be ordered today given the CRPC transition.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue grade 2, persistent. No fever, chills, drenching night sweats, or unintentional weight loss (weight stable at 91 kg versus 89 kg at prior visit).
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. Chronic low back discomfort unchanged from prior, attributed to known lumbar spine metastases, currently well controlled on scheduled ibuprofen PRN and acetaminophen.
- Neurologic: No extremity weakness, paresthesias, bowel/bladder changes, gait instability beyond what was noted on enzalutamide initiation note (addressed with PT referral). No seizure activity.
- Cardiovascular: Hot flashes grade 2-3, bothersome. Grade 1 hypertension, managed with amlodipine 5 mg daily (started at prior visit).
- GI: No nausea, vomiting. Appetite intact. No hematemesis or melena.
- GU: Urinary flow adequate; no hematuria. Libido absent (expected on ADT).
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding.
- Psychiatric: Patient reports mild cognitive sluggishness, which he attributes to ADT — has been ongoing for several months. No depression screened for today (PHQ-2 negative).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.8°C, Weight 91 kg.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

General: Well-appearing elderly male, in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and engaged.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Oropharynx clear.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. Mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+, pitting, to ankles bilaterally — present at prior visits, stable).
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites.
Musculoskeletal: No reproducible point tenderness over spine on percussion. No flank tenderness. Normal strength in bilateral upper and lower extremities.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait steady with cane (used since enzalutamide initiation for fall prevention).
Skin: No rash or petechiae.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY):
- PSA: 6.7 ng/mL (PSA trend: 1.8 → 3.4 → 6.7 ng/mL over ~12 weeks)
- Testosterone: 9 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
- CBC: WBC 6.8 × 10⁹/L, Hgb 11.4 g/dL, Plt 212 × 10⁹/L
- BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cr 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 66 mL/min/1.73m², BUN 18, Glucose 97
- LFTs: ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Alk Phos 98 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
- LDH: 188 U/L (upper normal)
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
- AR-V7 liquid biopsy (ctDNA): Ordered today — result pending

---

IMAGING REVIEW:
- Tc-99m bone scan (~6 weeks prior): Stable osseous metastatic disease in lumbar spine, pelvis, right femur. No new bone lesions. Previously documented stable since initiation of ADT.
- CT C/A/P with contrast: Most recent scan was at diagnosis, showing pelvic lymphadenopathy and no visceral disease. New CT ordered today for baseline cross-sectional restaging prior to next-line therapy.

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC) — NEW DIAGNOSIS
Mr. Delacroix has met formal criteria for CRPC based on serial PSA rises (1.8 → 3.4 → 6.7 ng/mL) in the setting of confirmed castrate testosterone (9 ng/dL), with PSA now >2 ng/mL and each rise ≥25% above nadir. Abiraterone (Zytiga)/prednisone will be discontinued. ADT with leuprolide will be continued — there is no established benefit to stopping ADT at CRPC transition, and ongoing testosterone suppression is the standard of care.

At this stage, he has biochemical-only progression without radiographic disease progression (no new lesions on recent bone scan, cross-sectional imaging pending). His ECOG PS remains 1 and he is functionally well, giving us some flexibility in treatment sequencing.

2. TREATMENT SELECTION FOR mCRPC:
The key decision point at this visit is sequencing between enzalutamide and olaparib, given his germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with confirmed tumor LOH.

Arguments for enzalutamide now:
- He has not been AR-V7 tested yet; pending result. If AR-V7 negative, he may still derive meaningful benefit from AR pathway inhibition (enzalutamide per PROSPER/AFFIRM data in the post-abiraterone setting, though cross-resistance is recognized).
- Olaparib (Lynparza) can be preserved for a subsequent line once he has exhausted or progressed on enzalutamide, as supported by its approval in the HRR-mutated mCRPC setting regardless of prior ARSI use.
- Enzalutamide has a well-characterized tolerability profile and does not carry the risk of myelosuppression — relevant given his Hgb is already borderline at 11.4 g/dL, likely ADT-related anemia.
- He has biochemical-only progression without visceral disease; the urgency to switch to chemotherapy-class or PARP inhibitor is not driven by visceral crisis at this juncture.

Arguments for upfront olaparib:
- Per PROfound and BRCA2 germline cohort data, patients with BRCA1/2 alterations have the strongest benefit from olaparib in the post-ARSI setting.
- Delaying olaparib risks disease progression to a state where PARP inhibitor efficacy may be diminished.
- However, with no radiographic progression and stable performance status, the window to use enzalutamide is still reasonable.

Decision: After discussing the data and treatment options at length with Mr. Delacroix and his wife, the patient expressed preference for a tablet regimen with a tolerability profile that allows him to remain active and continue his daily routine. He is aware that the BRCA2 mutation makes him an excellent candidate for olaparib and understands it is being held for next-line after enzalutamide. He wishes to defer olaparib and proceed with enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily, which will be initiated today. Enzalutamide has known activity in post-abiraterone mCRPC and is preferred over docetaxel at this point given his clear preference to avoid IV chemotherapy.

Clinical trial options were reviewed — no open relevant studies at our institution at this time. Patient declined referral to academic center for trial enrollment.

3. BONE-PROTECTIVE THERAPY:
Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (as initiated at 3-month post-diagnosis visit). Continue calcium 1200 mg daily and vitamin D 1000 IU daily. Dental clearance is up to date.

Chest wall and pleura: No pleural effusions. Bilateral gynecomastia noted, likely related to ongoing androgen deprivation therapy, stable in appearance. There are post-radiation changes in the lower thoracic spine (T10-T12 region) with expected soft tissue sclerosis corresponding to the prior radiation field. Known osseous metastatic lesions in the thoracic spine are discussed below.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is mildly enlarged with craniocaudal span of approximately 18 cm. There are two new hypodense lesions in the hepatic parenchyma that were not present on the prior CT. Segment 5 lesion: Irregular, hypodense mass measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm on portal venous phase imaging, with mild peripheral rim enhancement and central hypoattenuation (series 5, image 68). Segment 7 lesion: Hypodense, rounded lesion measuring 1.2 x 1.0 cm with similar enhancement characteristics (series 5, image 55). Both lesions demonstrate washout on delayed phase. No additional hepatic lesions identified on this study. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. No biliary obstruction.

Gallbladder and biliary: Gallbladder is unremarkable without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct is normal in caliber at 4 mm.

Pancreas: Pancreas is homogeneous in attenuation. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic fluid or mass.

Spleen: Spleen is normal in size at 11.4 cm. No focal lesions.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses. No change from prior.

Kidneys: Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. A 5 mm left renal cortical hypodensity is stable and consistent with a simple cyst.

Gastrointestinal tract: Stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel are unremarkable. No bowel wall thickening or obstruction. Appendix is not visualized separately but there is no periappendiceal inflammatory change.

Retroperitoneum and mesentery: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy beyond what is described in the pelvis. No free fluid.

PELVIS:

Prostate: The prostate gland remains markedly enlarged, with heterogeneous internal architecture. Seminal vesicles are abnormal bilaterally with architectural distortion, consistent with known invasion, unchanged in overall configuration. Post-treatment changes.

Lymph nodes: The right obturator lymph node has increased in size from 1.3 cm on the prior study to 1.9 cm on the current examination. The left external iliac node previously measured 1.4 cm and is now difficult to discretely identify amid adjacent soft tissue; a confluent soft tissue density in this region measures approximately 1.6 cm. No new discrete pelvic lymphadenopathy identified beyond these.

Bladder: Bladder is decompressed. No wall thickening or mass.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:

The previously documented sclerotic and mixed lytic-sclerotic osseous metastases in the lumbar spine (L2, L3, L4), pelvis (bilateral sacroiliac joints, right iliac wing), and right proximal femur are identified on this study. Several of these lesions appear slightly more conspicuous compared to the prior CT, with marginal increase in associated soft tissue density around the L3 vertebral body. No discrete new osseous lesions are identified on this CT; bone scan and/or PSMA PET would be more sensitive for evaluation of new osseous metastatic disease. No pathologic fractures. No epidural disease is assessed by CT alone; prior MRI spine is on file.

SOFT TISSUES: Mild bilateral lower extremity soft tissue edema suggested at the imaged inferior pelvis/upper thigh level. No subcutaneous masses.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Two new hepatic lesions in segments 5 and 7 (1.8 cm and 1.2 cm respectively), not present on prior imaging, with enhancement pattern raising strong concern for hepatic metastases in the setting of known metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer.

2. Two new small pulmonary nodules, right lower lobe (8 mm) and left lower lobe (6 mm), not present on prior CT. In the clinical context of advanced prostate cancer with progression, these are suspicious for pulmonary metastatic deposits.

3. Interval enlargement of the right obturator lymph node from 1.3 cm to 1.9 cm, indicating nodal progression.

4. Known osseous metastatic disease throughout the lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur appears at minimum stable to mildly more prominent at some sites; dedicated bone scintigraphy or PSMA PET would better characterize skeletal burden.

5. Post-radiation changes in the T10-T12 region, grossly stable.

6. Mild hepatomegaly noted.

Overall, these findings represent substantial interval change from prior imaging, with the development of new hepatic and pulmonary lesions most concerning for visceral metastatic spread in this patient with progressive CRPC.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

The hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained sections reveal three core biopsy fragments with partial preservation of normal hepatic architecture in a minority of the tissue. The majority of all three cores are replaced by an infiltrating carcinomatous process.

The neoplastic cells are arranged in a variety of architectural patterns, most prominently small to medium-sized acini, glandular tubules, and focal cribriform structures. The acinar structures are irregular, with luminal spaces occasionally containing pink amorphous secretions. Fused glands and poorly formed glandular units are present. The individual neoplastic cells are large, with abundant pale-to-eosinophilic cytoplasm. No neuroendocrine morphology is appreciated: no salt-and-pepper chromatin, no rosette formation, no organoid nesting, and no trabecular architecture suggestive of small cell or large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma.

The background hepatic parenchyma (present at one edge of a core fragment) shows mild sinusoidal dilatation and minor lobular architectural distortion, without significant inflammation, fibrosis, or bile duct abnormality. No steatosis or significant hepatocyte ballooning noted.

COMMENT:

The morphology and immunophenotype of this hepatic core biopsy are diagnostic of metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma. The acinar architecture with prominent nucleoli and high-grade cytology is consistent with the patient's known Gleason 9 (Grade Group 5) primary prostate cancer. No histologic evidence of small cell or large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma transformation is present on this specimen, which is a clinically important distinction given the patient's prior treatment with multiple lines of androgen receptor pathway inhibitors.

Androgen receptor expression is retained and strong, though AR splice variant activity (e.g., AR-V7) cannot be assessed by standard IHC on this specimen; this is best evaluated by dedicated molecular assay.

The high Ki-67 labeling index (~45%) is consistent with aggressive tumor biology in the setting of treatment-refractory mCRPC.

PTEN loss by IHC correlates with biallelic deletion previously identified on genomic profiling of the primary tumor and is consistent with the known aggressive clinical phenotype.

Correlation with clinical history, prior pathology (SP-[REDACTED], transrectal biopsy), and updated molecular profiling is recommended.

CLINICAL INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This comprehensive genomic profile was obtained from a liver metastasis biopsy at a time of documented radiographic and biochemical progression following ola (PARP inhibitor) therapy. Comparison with the previously reported prostate primary biopsy NGS profile reveals clonal evolution and acquisition of several therapeutically relevant alterations:

1. AR-V7 SPLICE VARIANT (NEW; not detected on primary biopsy): Detection of the androgen receptor variant 7 (ARv7) indicates a constitutively active truncated AR isoform lacking the ligand-binding domain. This is a well-established mechanism of resistance to both first- and second-generation androgen receptor signaling inhibitors (ARSIs), including enza and Zytiga. ARv7 positivity is correlated with poor response to further ARSI therapy. Taxane-based chemotherapy (doce or cabazitaxel) retains activity in ARv7-positive disease.

2. MET HIGH-LEVEL AMPLIFICATION (NEW; copy number 18): High-level MET amplification represents a newly acquired genomic alteration not present in the prostate primary specimen. This is a recognized acquired resistance mechanism in advanced prostate cancer and may contribute to ola resistance and ARSI-refractory disease. MET amplification may potentially confer sensitivity to MET-targeted therapies, though clinical data in prostate cancer remain limited.

3. AKT1 p.E17K MUTATION (NEW; VAF 8%): This sub-clonal AKT1 activating mutation at the pleckstrin homology domain results in constitutive PI3K-AKT pathway activation. Combined with the pre-existing PIK3CA p.E545K mutation and PTEN homozygous deletion (already present in the primary), this tumor harbors convergent activation of the PI3K-AKT pathway across multiple genomic nodes. The low VAF (8%) suggests this is a sub-clonal acquired event, possibly representing an emerging resistant clone under selective pressure.

4. BRCA2 BIALLELIC LOSS (CONFIRMED; germline + somatic LOH): Biallelic inactivation of BRCA2 is maintained from the primary tumor and confirmed in this metastatic deposit. BRCA2 biallelic loss should confer sensitivity to PARP inhibitors; however, disease is progressing on Lynparza, suggesting additional resistance mechanisms have supervened (ARv7, MET amplification, AKT1 pathway). Notably, no BRCA2 reversion mutations were identified in this specimen — ola resistance in this case does not appear to be driven by BRCA2 restoration.

5. CDK12 BIALLELIC INACTIVATION: CDK12 p.K1213fs*6 (VAF 34%) is enriched from the primary (VAF 18%), consistent with clonal expansion. CDK12 biallelic inactivation in prostate cancer has been associated with focal tandem duplications (FTDs) genome-wide, and a subset of CDK12-deficient prostate cancers may exhibit a distinct mutational signature (tandem duplications rather than high somatic TMB). CDK12 biallelic inactivation has been proposed as a potential predictor of benefit from immunotherapy combinations in some preliminary reports, although TMB remains low (6 mut/Mb) and MSI status is stable (MSS) in this case.

6. TP53 p.R248W, RB1 SPLICE VARIANT, PIK3CA p.E545K, AR AMPLIFICATION, PTEN DELETION, MYC AMPLIFICATION: All clonal alterations present in the primary tumor are retained and enriched (higher VAFs) in the metastatic deposit, consistent with their founding clonal status and ongoing selective pressure. AR amplification has increased from copy number 8 to 14, further supporting AR-driven resistance.

SUMMARY STATEMENT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Comprehensive genomic profiling of this hepatic metastasis from a patient with heavily pre-treated castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma identifies a complex, clonally evolved tumor genome. The primary clonal architecture (TP53, PTEN, CDK12, RB1, PIK3CA, AR amplification, MYC amplification) is maintained and expanded from the initial prostate primary. Critically, this metastatic specimen demonstrates three newly acquired or enriched resistance-associated alterations: (1) AR-V7 splice variant positivity, predicting resistance to ARSI-based therapies; (2) high-level MET amplification (CN 18), a recognized acquired resistance mechanism; and (3) a sub-clonal AKT1 p.E17K activating mutation, adding convergent PI3K-AKT pathway activation. BRCA2 biallelic loss is confirmed without evidence of reversion mutation, indicating that Lynparza resistance in this case is not attributable to BRCA2 restoration. The overall genomic landscape is consistent with a highly aggressive, genomically complex CRPC with multiple co-occurring resistance mechanisms.

These findings have been reviewed by the molecular tumor board. Clinical correlation and multidisciplinary discussion are warranted. Clinical trial enrollment should be considered.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Robert Delacroix is a 72-year-old French-Canadian male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for routine follow-up approximately 3 weeks after his second cycle of lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto). He reports feeling "a little less exhausted" compared to prior visits, though fatigue remains a significant issue. He also notes persistent dry mouth.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delacroix was initially diagnosed with high-grade, metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (Gleason 9, cT3bN1M1b) approximately four years ago. His disease course has been extensive: he received ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga) as frontline therapy with an excellent PSA response (nadir 0.8 ng/mL), followed by progression to castration-resistant disease at roughly 18 months. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi), achieving a PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL, but developed new bone metastases at T10, T12, and the right 4th rib at approximately 9 months. Genomic testing (germline) confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 mutation, and he was switched to olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID, later dose-reduced to 200 mg BID due to grade 3 anemia requiring transfusions. TECHNETIUM-99M METHYLENE DIPHOSPHONATE (Tc-99m MDP) WHOLE BODY BONE SCAN

INDICATION:
71-year-old male (DOB on file) with newly diagnosed high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, bilateral involvement with extraprostatic extension and seminal vesicle invasion. PSA at diagnosis 87.4 ng/mL. Staging bone scan to evaluate for osseous metastatic disease prior to initiation of systemic therapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Intravenous administration of approximately 25 mCi (925 MBq) Tc-99m MDP. Imaging performed approximately 3 hours post-injection. Whole body planar scintigraphy obtained in anterior and posterior projections from skull vertex to toes. Supplemental spot images of the pelvis, lumbar spine, and right lower extremity obtained. SPECT/CT of the pelvis and lumbar spine performed for anatomic localization of equivocal findings.

FINDINGS:

SKULL/CERVICAL SPINE:
No focal areas of abnormal radiotracer accumulation in the calvarium or cervical spine. Symmetric physiologic distribution of tracer activity in the facial bones and skull base.

THORACIC SPINE AND RIBS:
Focal area of mildly increased radiotracer uptake in the posterior aspect of the left 7th rib, at the posterior axillary line. The lesion is discrete, not associated with any overlying skin or soft tissue abnormality on SPECT/CT. No additional rib lesions identified. Thoracic spine shows no convincing focal areas of increased uptake. No evidence of diffuse rib involvement.

LUMBAR SPINE:
Multiple discrete foci of moderately to markedly increased radiotracer uptake involving the L2, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies. On SPECT/CT correlation, these correspond to mixed lytic-sclerotic changes within the vertebral bodies. The L1 and L5 vertebral bodies appear grossly unremarkable. No involvement of the posterior elements at these levels is definitively identified.

PELVIS AND SACRUM:
Intensely increased radiotracer uptake involving the bilateral sacroiliac joints, with involvement appearing somewhat greater on the right than the left. The right iliac wing demonstrates a discrete focus of moderately increased uptake in its mid-posterior aspect. Additional foci of increased uptake are present in the right superior pubic ramus region. The left hemipelvis and left iliac wing do not demonstrate convincing additional focal lesions beyond the sacroiliac joint involvement. SPECT/CT correlation confirms these correspond to areas of mixed sclerotic bony change rather than degenerative joint disease.

RIGHT LOWER EXTREMITY:
Prominent increased radiotracer uptake at the right femoral neck extending into the proximal femoral shaft, consistent with an osseous lesion. On SPECT/CT, this corresponds to a zone of mixed sclerotic and lytic change without cortical disruption. No abnormal uptake in the femoral condyles or distal right lower extremity. The left femur and both tibiae/fibulae are unremarkable.

REMAINDER OF SKELETON:
The visualized appendicular skeleton including shoulders, humeri, elbows, wrists, left femur, bilateral knees, ankles, and feet are unremarkable without focal areas of abnormal uptake. The sternum and manubrium appear normal.

REMARKS ON SUPERSCAN:
Despite the extent of osseous involvement, the overall image quality is preserved with adequate bladder/renal activity visualized. There is no diffuse skeletal uptake to suggest a superscan pattern, and the kidneys are clearly identified bilaterally. The hepatic and splenic activity is within normal limits.

SOFT TISSUE/BACKGROUND:
Background soft tissue radiotracer distribution is within normal limits. Physiologic tracer activity in the kidneys and bladder. No abnormal soft tissue foci of uptake identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Extensive polyostotic skeletal metastatic disease involving multiple axial and appendicular sites. Sites with convincing findings for osseous metastatic involvement include: bilateral sacroiliac joints (right greater than left), L2, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, posterior left 7th rib, and right femoral neck and proximal shaft.

2. The distribution and character of the lesions -- particularly the axial-predominant pattern with involvement at the right femoral neck -- is consistent with the known high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma and markedly elevated serum PSA of 87.4 ng/mL.

3. No superscan. No convincing evidence of thoracic spine, cervical spine, or extensive appendicular skeletal involvement at this time, though a small number of lesions may be below scintigraphic detection threshold.

4. Correlation with cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI) is recommended for complete anatomic characterization, particularly given the extent of pelvic disease and the right femoral neck lesion with respect to fracture risk assessment.

Germline testing performed on peripheral blood-derived DNA using a targeted hereditary cancer panel encompassing BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, FANCA, FANCC, FANCL, NBN, RAD51C, RAD51D, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and additional HRR/MMR genes.

*** PATHOGENIC GERMLINE VARIANT IDENTIFIED ***

Gene:        BRCA2
Variant:     c.5946delT (p.Ser1982Argfs*22)
Zygosity:    Heterozygous (germline)
Classification: PATHOGENIC (ClinVar: RCV000031443; known founder variant)
Inheritance: Autosomal dominant
Penetrance:  High penetrance for breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and prostate cancer

Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH) Assessment in Tumor:
BRCA2 somatic second hit confirmed — loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at BRCA2 locus detected in tumor tissue, indicating biallelic inactivation of BRCA2 in the malignant clone. This constitutes a classic two-hit mechanism (Knudson model).

Additional Germline HRR/MMR Genes Reviewed:
BRCA1: No pathogenic variant detected
ATM: No pathogenic variant detected
PALB2: No pathogenic variant detected
CHEK2: c.1100delC NOT detected; no other pathogenic CHEK2 variant
FANCA, FANCC, FANCL: No pathogenic variants detected
MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2: No pathogenic variants detected
RAD51C, RAD51D: No pathogenic variants detected

Note: Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) in BRCA2 and other genes, if identified, are reported in the supplementary VUS table. No VUS with sufficient evidence to alter clinical management identified at this time.

Genetic counseling referral: STRONGLY RECOMMENDED. This germline finding has implications for the patient and for first- and second-degree family members. Cascade testing should be offered to adult relatives.

========================================
INTERPRETATION & CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
========================================

TIER I — Strong Clinical Significance (Well-established clinical relevance)

1. BRCA2 Germline Pathogenic Variant (c.5946delT) + Somatic LOH (Biallelic Inactivation)
   Interpretation: Germline BRCA2 loss with confirmed somatic second hit. Biallelic BRCA2 inactivation renders tumor dependent on alternative DNA repair pathways (NHEJ), creating synthetic lethality with PARP inhibitors. This patient is potentially eligible for PARP inhibitor therapy (e.g., olaparib, rucaparib) per FDA-approved indications in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with homologous recombination repair (HRR) gene mutations. At present (castration-sensitive disease), PARP inhibitor therapy is not yet standard; however, this finding should be considered proactively for treatment sequencing at the time of CRPC transition.
   Germline counseling referral placed per NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment guidelines.

2. PTEN Homozygous Deletion
   Interpretation: Biallelic PTEN loss is one of the most common alterations in high-grade prostate cancer and is associated with aggressive disease biology, poor prognosis, and activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. PTEN loss in the context of AR-positive prostate cancer co-activates the PI3K pathway via reciprocal feedback with AR signaling. Combination PI3K/AKT pathway inhibition (e.g., ipatasertib, capivasertib) with AR-directed therapy is under active clinical investigation. This finding also confirms high-risk disease and may inform intensity of systemic therapy.

3. AR Amplification (CN ~8)
   Interpretation: AR gene amplification is a known mechanism of treatment resistance, particularly to androgen deprivation therapy. While detected in a castration-sensitive setting, AR amplification suggests a significant AR-driven tumor biology and may predict for earlier development of castration resistance. AR amplification should be noted in the context of treatment sequencing.

TIER II — Potential Clinical Significance (Emerging or context-dependent relevance)

4. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 42%, Clonal)
   Interpretation: TP53 R248W is a gain-of-function hotspot mutation in the DNA-binding domain of p53. As a clonal, likely truncal mutation, this is consistent with early acquisition and likely plays a driver role. TP53 mutation is associated with genomic instability, aggressive disease behavior, and reduced response to certain therapies. No direct TP53-targeted agents are currently approved for prostate cancer. This finding reinforces the high-risk disease designation.

5. CDK12 p.K1213fs*6 (Biallelic Inactivation — see below)
   Interpretation: CDK12 encodes a cyclin-dependent kinase essential for transcription-coupled DNA repair. CDK12 biallelic inactivation is found in approximately 5-7% of metastatic prostate cancers and is associated with a distinctive genomic signature of focal tandem duplications (FTDs), yielding a unique class of neoantigens. CDK12-mutated prostate cancers have shown preliminary signals of benefit from immune checkpoint blockade (pembrolizumab) in small series and warrant consideration of immunotherapy in appropriate clinical settings, despite low TMB and MSS status. CDK12 biallelic inactivation status: NOTE — a second somatic hit in CDK12 is needed to confirm biallelic loss. The frameshift at VAF 18% combined with copy number analysis will be reviewed; allelic loss assessment is pending supplementary analysis (see footnote). If biallelic, CDK12 inactivation may qualify for immunotherapy trials.

6. RB1 Splice Site Mutation (c.607-2A>G, VAF 31%)
   Interpretation: RB1 loss is associated with lineage plasticity and risk of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation in prostate cancer treated with potent AR-directed therapies. Pathologists and clinicians should maintain vigilance for phenotypic evolution (small cell / neuroendocrine prostate cancer) at the time of disease progression, particularly if AR-directed resistance is observed.

7. PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 12%, Subclonal)
   Interpretation: PIK3CA helical domain activating mutation, subclonal. PI3K pathway activation is consistent with PTEN loss in this tumor. Subclonal nature may limit overall tumor dependency on PIK3CA. PI3K alpha-selective inhibitors (alpelisib) are not approved in prostate cancer but trials are ongoing.

8. MYC Amplification (CN ~12)
   Interpretation: MYC amplification is a common copy number gain in high-grade prostate cancer and is associated with aggressive disease biology. No directly approved MYC-targeted therapies exist; however, this finding contributes to the overall picture of a genomically complex, high-risk tumor.

========================================
SUMMARY OF KEY ACTIONABLE FINDINGS
========================================
- BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant with biallelic tumor inactivation: PARP inhibitor eligibility at mCRPC (olaparib per PROfound trial eligibility criteria; rucaparib per TRITON2/3 data)
- PTEN homozygous loss: PI3K/AKT pathway activation; potential eligibility for AKT inhibitor combinations in clinical trials
- AR amplification: Monitoring for early CRPC; may inform treatment selection
- CDK12 biallelic inactivation (pending confirmation): Potential immunotherapy consideration
- RB1 loss: Vigilance for neuroendocrine transdifferentiation at progression
- MSS, low TMB (4 mut/Mb): Checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy not indicated based on these biomarkers alone
- AR-V7: Not detected at this time point

========================================
LIMITATIONS
========================================
This assay detects variants with VAF >=5% (for point mutations/indels) and high-confidence CNVs in tumor regions. This analysis is not intended for the detection of all possible variants, and false-negatives may occur in heterogeneous, low-cellularity samples. Germline findings are limited to variants within the tested gene panel; a negative result does not exclude the possibility of pathogenic variants in untested regions or genes. This report should be interpreted in the clinical context by a qualified physician. Therapeutic recommendations should be made in conjunction with current clinical guidelines and individual patient factors.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delacroix was initially diagnosed approximately 12 months ago with de novo metastatic prostate cancer (PSA at diagnosis 87.4 ng/mL) with extensive osseous involvement — lumbar spine (L2-L4), bilateral sacroiliac joints, right femoral neck/proximal shaft, left 7th posterior rib — as well as pelvic lymphadenopathy (right obturator 2.1 cm, left external iliac 1.4 cm). Transrectal biopsy confirmed high-grade acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, involving all 12 cores bilaterally with perineural invasion and bilateral seminal vesicle involvement on staging CT.

He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3 months plus abiraterone (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID approximately 12 months ago for mCSPC. At his 3-month visit, PSA had declined dramatically from 87.4 to 3.2 ng/mL (96% decline), testosterone confirmed castrate at 14 ng/dL. Zoledronic acid was added at that time for bone-directed therapy along with calcium/vitamin D supplementation.

NGS was performed on FFPE biopsy tissue and peripheral blood germline testing at the time of diagnosis. Notably, germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) was confirmed, with biallelic loss in tumor (somatic LOH). Somatic profile included TP53 p.R248W, homozygous PTEN deletion, CDK12 frameshift, RB1 splice site mutation, PIK3CA p.E545K, and AR amplification (CN 8). MSS, TMB 4 mut/Mb. Genetic counseling referral was placed. Family members have been informed of hereditary implications.

Today at 12 months on regimen, the patient returns for routine follow-up. He reports persistent but manageable hot flashes, ongoing mild fatigue, and new subjective cognitive sluggishness that he describes as "brain fog" — difficulty finding words during conversations and occasional short-term memory lapses. He attributes this partly to poor sleep from nocturnal hot flashes. He denies new bone pain, urinary symptoms, lower extremity weakness or numbness, or fevers. He has been adherent to oral abiraterone and prednisone, confirming he takes Zytiga on an empty stomach at the same time each day.

Bone scan performed recently (interval ~18 months from diagnosis, ~6 months prior to today's visit) showed stable osseous metastases without new lesions. A repeat bone scan was performed approximately 2 weeks ago at the 12-month mark — results reviewed today (see imaging below).

NUCLEAR MEDICINE BONE SCINTIGRAPHY
TECHNETIUM-99M METHYLENE DIPHOSPHONATE (Tc-99m MDP) WHOLE BODY BONE SCAN

INDICATION: 70-year-old male with known metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (Gleason 9, Stage IVB, initial PSA 87.4 ng/mL). Currently on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide plus abiraterone acetate/prednisone. Restaging at approximately 18 months of therapy. Prior bone scan (at diagnosis, ~18 months ago) demonstrated polyostotic skeletal metastatic disease involving bilateral sacroiliac joints, L2-L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, right femoral neck and proximal shaft, and left 7th posterior rib.

TECHNIQUE: Intravenous injection of 25.5 mCi (943 MBq) Tc-99m MDP. Whole-body planar images acquired in anterior and posterior projections approximately 3 hours post-injection using a dual-headed gamma camera. Additional spot views of the spine, pelvis, and right lower extremity obtained. SPECT/CT of the lumbar spine and pelvis performed for anatomic correlation.

COMPARISON: Whole-body bone scan from approximately 18 months prior (at initial staging).

FINDINGS:

SKULL AND CERVICAL SPINE: No abnormal radiotracer uptake identified. No new foci of increased activity.

THORACIC SPINE: No focal areas of abnormal increased radiotracer accumulation. Previously described lesions were not in the thoracic spine; no new thoracic involvement seen on this examination.

LUMBAR SPINE: Persistent heterogeneous radiotracer uptake in the L2, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies, consistent with known osseous metastatic involvement. The distribution and intensity of uptake in these vertebral bodies appear comparable to the prior examination without convincing increase or new extension. SPECT/CT confirms sclerotic changes at L2 and L3 with corresponding radiotracer uptake in keeping with treated/stable metastatic lesions.

PELVIS AND SACRUM: Persistent increased radiotracer uptake in the bilateral sacroiliac regions, similar in distribution and intensity to prior study. The right iliac wing continues to demonstrate focal increased activity in a pattern consistent with the previously documented metastatic lesion. No new pelvic foci of concern. The sacrum itself shows no new lesions. Uptake at the bilateral sacroiliac joints is symmetric and not significantly changed.

RIB CAGE AND STERNUM: Persistent focal increased uptake in the left 7th posterior rib, in keeping with the known metastatic lesion at this site. No convincing change in intensity. No new rib lesions identified bilaterally. No sternal involvement.

SHOULDERS AND UPPER EXTREMITIES: No abnormal focal radiotracer uptake.

RIGHT FEMUR: Persistent increased radiotracer uptake in the right femoral neck and proximal shaft, consistent with the known metastatic disease at this site and unchanged from prior examination. The extent of uptake in the right femoral neck does not appear significantly progressed. No new femoral lesions. Left femur appears normal.

KNEES AND LOWER EXTREMITIES: Symmetric physiologic uptake in the bilateral knees and lower extremities. No focal abnormalities.

SOFT TISSUE: No pathologic soft tissue radiotracer uptake to suggest extraosseous disease or soft tissue metastases. Kidneys demonstrate symmetric excretion. Bladder activity as expected.

GENERAL SKELETAL SURVEY: The background skeletal uptake is appropriate. No superscan pattern. No diffuse abnormal uptake. Overall bone mineralization appears adequate.

IMPRESSION:

1. Known osseous metastatic lesions in the lumbar spine (L2, L3, L4), bilateral sacroiliac regions, right iliac wing, right femoral neck/proximal shaft, and left 7th posterior rib are all stable in appearance compared to the prior bone scan from 18 months ago. No convincing progression in the extent or intensity of uptake at any previously identified site.

2. No new bone metastases identified anywhere in the axial or appendicular skeleton.

3. No suspicious soft tissue radiotracer accumulation.

4. Findings are consistent with stable skeletal disease in the setting of ongoing systemic therapy. Sclerotic changes on correlative SPECT/CT at lumbar spine lesions are in keeping with treatment-related osseous remodeling.
